title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an architect called Ada Thorne talking about the challenges of designing buildings to withstand natural disasters. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an architect called Ada Thorne talking about the challenges of designing buildings to withstand natural disasters. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Ada Thorne discusses earthquake-resistant design and the use of sustainable materials in high-risk zones.
extracts.audio_script:
Hello. I’m Ada. When we think of architecture, we often focus on the aesthetics—the way a building looks in the sunlight. But for me, the most beautiful thing about a building is its resilience. I specialize in disaster-resistant architecture. My interest in this field didn't start in a classroom; it actually began when I volunteered at a [7]reconstruction project[/7] after a major earthquake in Chile. Seeing how easily traditional buildings had collapsed made me realize that good design is a matter of life and death.

Designing for earthquake zones is about flexibility, not strength. If a building is too rigid, it will snap under the pressure. I’ve been working with 'base isolation' systems, which are essentially [8]rubber pads[/8] placed between the building and its foundation. They act like shock absorbers in a car, allowing the ground to move while the structure remains relatively still. It’s an expensive technology, but it’s becoming the standard for hospitals and emergency centers.

However, it’s not just about the foundation. The shape of the building also matters. I’ve found that a [9]circular floorplan[/9] is much better at distributing the energy of a seismic wave than a traditional rectangle. It’s a concept we’ve borrowed from ancient Japanese pagodas, which have survived for centuries in one of the most active earthquake zones in the world. We are constantly learning from the past to protect our future.

In recent years, we’ve also had to consider the impact of rising sea levels and flooding. We are experimenting with 'amphibious' houses. These are buildings that sit on the ground but are designed to float when the water rises. I noticed that the [10]buoyancy system[/10] is the most difficult part of the engineering. You have to ensure the house remains level even if the water is moving rapidly. It’s a fascinating blend of naval architecture and civil engineering.

Materials are also changing. We are moving away from heavy concrete and steel towards 'cross-laminated timber' or CLT. I was surprised to find that this specially treated wood has a [11]fire resistance[/11] that is actually superior to unprotected steel. When steel gets hot, it melts and loses its structural integrity; CLT chars on the outside but maintains its strength on the inside. Plus, it’s a much more sustainable way to build.

Working in this field requires a lot of collaboration. I spend a lot of time in laboratories testing new materials. I’ve found that the [12]wind tunnel tests[/12] are the most stressful part of the design process. Watching a model of your building being subjected to hurricane-force winds is nerve-wracking, but it’s the only way to ensure the safety of the real structure. One small miscalculation in the roof's angle can lead to total failure.

Looking back, the biggest change I’ve seen is in the [13]public awareness[/13] of climate-related risks. People are no longer just looking for a beautiful home; they are looking for a safe one. They want to know that their investment will still be standing in fifty years. This demand is driving a lot of innovation in the private sector.

Finally, I’m currently writing a [14]technical manual[/14] for architects in developing countries. I want to share low-cost techniques that can be used with local materials like bamboo to create safer housing. Everyone deserves a home that can withstand the elements. Thank you.
